attribution.note: English translation (public domain via Project Gutenberg)

name: The Art of War — Sun Tzu
description:
The foundational text on strategy and warfare by Sun Tzu (544-496 BCE), Chinese military general, strategist, and philosopher. Thirteen chapters covering strategic planning, tactical dispositions, energy, terrain, espionage, and the art of winning without fighting. 'Supreme excellence consists in breaking the enemy's resistance without fighting.' Lionel Giles translation (1910), the definitive English edition. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #132 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/132). Commentary and footnotes by Giles have been stripped — only Sun Tzu's original verses are included.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese calligraphy and brush paintings of the Art of War text. Bamboo slip manuscripts — the earliest surviving copies of Sun Tzu were written on bamboo strips (Yinqueshan Han Slips, 2nd century BCE). Chinese landscape paintings from the Song and Ming dynasties depicting military campaigns. Illustrations from early printed editions of the Art of War (Chinese woodblock prints). Japanese ukiyo-e prints depicting military strategy (Utagawa school). European military maps and battle diagrams from 18th-19th century editions.

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: The art of war...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: The art of war is of vital importance to the State.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is a matter of life and...

sections.Verse: It is a matter of life and death, a road either to safety or to ruin. Hence it is a subject of inquiry which can on no account be neglected.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The art of war, then, is governed...

sections.Verse: The art of war, then, is governed by five constant factors, to be taken into account in one’s deliberations, when seeking to determine the conditions obtaining in the field.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These are: (1) The Moral Law; (2)...

sections.Verse: These are: (1) The Moral Law; (2) Heaven; (3) Earth; (4) The Commander; (5) Method and discipline.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By Method and discipline are to be...

sections.Verse: By Method and discipline are to be understood the marshalling of the army in its proper subdivisions, the gradations of rank among the officers, the maintenance of roads by which supplies may reach the army, and the control of military expenditure.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These five heads should be familiar to...

sections.Verse: These five heads should be familiar to every general: he who knows them will be victorious; he who knows them not will fail.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore, in your deliberations, when seeking to...

sections.Verse: Therefore, in your deliberations, when seeking to determine the military conditions, let them be made the basis of a comparison, in this wise:—

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (1) Which of the two sovereigns is...

sections.Verse: (1) Which of the two sovereigns is imbued with the Moral law?

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By means of these seven considerations I...

sections.Verse: By means of these seven considerations I can forecast victory or defeat.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The general that hearkens to my counsel...

sections.Verse: The general that hearkens to my counsel and acts upon it, will conquer:—let such a one be retained in command! The general that hearkens not to my counsel nor acts upon it, will suffer defeat:—let such a one be dismissed!

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: While heeding the profit of my counsel,...

sections.Verse: While heeding the profit of my counsel, avail yourself also of any helpful circumstances over and beyond the ordinary rules.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: According as circumstances are favourable, one should...

sections.Verse: According as circumstances are favourable, one should modify one’s plans.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: All warfare is based on deception.

sections.Verse: All warfare is based on deception.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence, when able to attack, we must...

sections.Verse: Hence, when able to attack, we must seem unable; when using our forces, we must seem inactive; when we are near, we must make the enemy believe we are far away; when far away, we must make him believe we are near.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hold out baits to entice the enemy....

sections.Verse: Hold out baits to entice the enemy. Feign disorder, and crush him.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If he is secure at all points,...

sections.Verse: If he is secure at all points, be prepared for him. If he is in superior strength, evade him.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If your opponent is of choleric temper,...

sections.Verse: If your opponent is of choleric temper, seek to irritate him. Pretend to be weak, that he may grow arrogant.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If he is taking his ease, give...

sections.Verse: If he is taking his ease, give him no rest.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Attack him where he is unprepared, appear...

sections.Verse: Attack him where he is unprepared, appear where you are not expected.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These military devices, leading to victory, must...

sections.Verse: These military devices, leading to victory, must not be divulged beforehand.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now the general who wins a battle...

sections.Verse: Now the general who wins a battle makes many calculations in his temple ere the battle is fought.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: In the operations of...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: In the operations of war, where there are in the field a thousand swift chariots, as many heavy chariots, and a hundred thousand mail-clad soldiers,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you engage in actual fighting, if...

sections.Verse: When you engage in actual fighting, if victory is long in coming, the men’s weapons will grow dull and their ardour will be damped. If you lay siege to a town, you will exhaust your strength.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Again, if the campaign is protracted, the...

sections.Verse: Again, if the campaign is protracted, the resources of the State will not be equal to the strain.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now, when your weapons are dulled, your...

sections.Verse: Now, when your weapons are dulled, your ardour damped, your strength exhausted and your treasure spent, other chieftains will spring up to take advantage of your extremity. Then no man, however wise, will be able to avert the consequences that must ensue.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus, though we have heard of stupid...

sections.Verse: Thus, though we have heard of stupid haste in war, cleverness has never been seen associated with long delays.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There is no instance of a country...

sections.Verse: There is no instance of a country having benefited from prolonged warfare.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is only one who is thoroughly...

sections.Verse: It is only one who is thoroughly acquainted with the evils of war that can thoroughly understand the profitable way of carrying it on.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The skilful soldier does not raise a...

sections.Verse: The skilful soldier does not raise a second levy, neither are his supply-waggons loaded more than twice.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Bring war material with you from home,...

sections.Verse: Bring war material with you from home, but forage on the enemy. Thus the army will have food enough for its needs.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Poverty of the State exchequer causes an...

sections.Verse: Poverty of the State exchequer causes an army to be maintained by contributions from a distance. Contributing to maintain an army at a distance causes the people to be impoverished.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On the other hand, the proximity of...

sections.Verse: On the other hand, the proximity of an army causes prices to go up; and high prices cause the people’s substance to be drained away.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When their substance is drained away, the...

sections.Verse: When their substance is drained away, the peasantry will be afflicted by heavy exactions.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: With this loss of substance and exhaustion...

sections.Verse: With this loss of substance and exhaustion of strength, the homes of the people will be stripped bare, and three-tenths of their incomes will be dissipated;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence a wise general makes a point...

sections.Verse: Hence a wise general makes a point of foraging on the enemy. One cartload of the enemy’s provisions is equivalent to twenty of one’s own, and likewise a single picul of his provender is equivalent to twenty from one’s own store.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now in order to kill the enemy,...

sections.Verse: Now in order to kill the enemy, our men must be roused to anger; that there may be advantage from defeating the enemy, they must have their rewards.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore in chariot fighting, when ten or...

sections.Verse: Therefore in chariot fighting, when ten or more chariots have been taken, those should be rewarded who took the first. Our own flags should be substituted for those of the enemy, and the chariots mingled and used in conjunction with ours. The captured soldiers should be kindly treated and kept.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: This is called, using the conquered foe...

sections.Verse: This is called, using the conquered foe to augment one’s own strength.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In war, then, let your great object...

sections.Verse: In war, then, let your great object be victory, not lengthy campaigns.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus it may be known that the...

sections.Verse: Thus it may be known that the leader of armies is the arbiter of the people’s fate, the man on whom it depends whether the nation shall be in peace or in peril.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: In the practical art...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: In the practical art of war, the best thing of all is to take the enemy’s country whole and intact; to shatter and destroy it is not so good. So, too, it is better to capture an army entire than to destroy it, to capture a regiment, a detachment or a company entire than to destroy them.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence to fight and conquer in all...

sections.Verse: Hence to fight and conquer in all your battles is not supreme excellence; supreme excellence consists in breaking the enemy’s resistance without fighting.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus the highest form of generalship is...

sections.Verse: Thus the highest form of generalship is to baulk the enemy’s plans;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The rule is, not to besiege walled...

sections.Verse: The rule is, not to besiege walled cities if it can possibly be avoided.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The general, unable to control his irritation,...

sections.Verse: The general, unable to control his irritation, will launch his men to the assault like swarming ants,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore the skilful leader subdues the enemy’s...

sections.Verse: Therefore the skilful leader subdues the enemy’s troops without any fighting; he captures their cities without laying siege to them; he overthrows their kingdom without lengthy operations in the field.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: With his forces intact he will dispute...

sections.Verse: With his forces intact he will dispute the mastery of the Empire, and thus, without losing a man, his triumph will be complete.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is the rule in war, if...

sections.Verse: It is the rule in war, if our forces are ten to the enemy’s one, to surround him; if five to one, to attack him;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If equally matched, we can offer battle;

sections.Verse: If equally matched, we can offer battle;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence, though an obstinate fight may be...

sections.Verse: Hence, though an obstinate fight may be made by a small force, in the end it must be captured by the larger force.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now the general is the bulwark of...

sections.Verse: Now the general is the bulwark of the State: if the bulwark is complete at all points; the State will be strong; if the bulwark is defective, the State will be weak.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There are three ways in which a...

sections.Verse: There are three ways in which a ruler can bring misfortune upon his army:—

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (1) By commanding the army to advance...

sections.Verse: (1) By commanding the army to advance or to retreat, being ignorant of the fact that it cannot obey. This is called hobbling the army.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (2) By attempting to govern an army...

sections.Verse: (2) By attempting to govern an army in the same way as he administers a kingdom, being ignorant of the conditions which obtain in an army. This causes restlessness in the soldier’s minds.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (3) By employing the officers of his...

sections.Verse: (3) By employing the officers of his army without discrimination,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: But when the army is restless and...

sections.Verse: But when the army is restless and distrustful, trouble is sure to come from the other feudal princes. This is simply bringing anarchy into the army, and flinging victory away.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus we may know that there are...

sections.Verse: Thus we may know that there are five essentials for victory: (1) He will win who knows when to fight and when not to fight.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the saying: If you know the...

sections.Verse: Hence the saying: If you know the enemy and know yourself, you need not fear the result of a hundred battles. If you know yourself but not the enemy, for every victory gained you will also suffer a defeat.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: The good fighters of...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: The good fighters of old first put themselves beyond the possibility of defeat, and then waited for an opportunity of defeating the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To secure ourselves against defeat lies in...

sections.Verse: To secure ourselves against defeat lies in our own hands, but the opportunity of defeating the enemy is provided by the enemy himself.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus the good fighter is able to...

sections.Verse: Thus the good fighter is able to secure himself against defeat,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the saying: One may know how...

sections.Verse: Hence the saying: One may know how to conquer without being able to do it.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Security against defeat implies defensive tactics; ability...

sections.Verse: Security against defeat implies defensive tactics; ability to defeat the enemy means taking the offensive.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Standing on the defensive indicates insufficient strength;...

sections.Verse: Standing on the defensive indicates insufficient strength; attacking, a superabundance of strength.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The general who is skilled in defence...

sections.Verse: The general who is skilled in defence hides in the most secret recesses of the earth;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To see victory only when it is...

sections.Verse: To see victory only when it is within the ken of the common herd is not the acme of excellence.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Neither is it the acme of excellence...

sections.Verse: Neither is it the acme of excellence if you fight and conquer and the whole Empire says, "Well done!"

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To lift an autumn hair is no...

sections.Verse: To lift an autumn hair is no sign of great strength;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: What the ancients called a clever fighter...

sections.Verse: What the ancients called a clever fighter is one who not only wins, but excels in winning with ease.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence his victories bring him neither reputation...

sections.Verse: Hence his victories bring him neither reputation for wisdom nor credit for courage.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He wins his battles by making no...

sections.Verse: He wins his battles by making no mistakes.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the skilful fighter puts himself into...

sections.Verse: Hence the skilful fighter puts himself into a position which makes defeat impossible, and does not miss the moment for defeating the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus it is that in war the...

sections.Verse: Thus it is that in war the victorious strategist only seeks battle after the victory has been won, whereas he who is destined to defeat first fights and afterwards looks for victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The consummate leader cultivates the moral law,...

sections.Verse: The consummate leader cultivates the moral law, and strictly adheres to method and discipline; thus it is in his power to control success.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In respect of military method, we have,...

sections.Verse: In respect of military method, we have, firstly, Measurement; secondly, Estimation of quantity; thirdly, Calculation; fourthly, Balancing of chances; fifthly, Victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Measurement owes its existence to Earth; Estimation...

sections.Verse: Measurement owes its existence to Earth; Estimation of quantity to Measurement; Calculation to Estimation of quantity; Balancing of chances to Calculation; and Victory to Balancing of chances.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: A victorious army opposed to a routed...

sections.Verse: A victorious army opposed to a routed one, is as a pound’s weight placed in the scale against a single grain.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The onrush of a conquering force is...

sections.Verse: The onrush of a conquering force is like the bursting of pent-up waters into a chasm a thousand fathoms deep. So much for tactical dispositions.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: The control of a...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: The control of a large force is the same principle as the control of a few men: it is merely a question of dividing up their numbers.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Fighting with a large army under your...

sections.Verse: Fighting with a large army under your command is nowise different from fighting with a small one: it is merely a question of instituting signs and signals.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To ensure that your whole host may...

sections.Verse: To ensure that your whole host may withstand the brunt of the enemy’s attack and remain unshaken—this is effected by manœuvers direct and indirect.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: That the impact of your army may...

sections.Verse: That the impact of your army may be like a grindstone dashed against an egg—this is effected by the science of weak points and strong.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In all fighting, the direct method may...

sections.Verse: In all fighting, the direct method may be used for joining battle, but indirect methods will be needed in order to secure victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Indirect tactics, efficiently applied, are inexhausible as...

sections.Verse: Indirect tactics, efficiently applied, are inexhausible as Heaven and Earth, unending as the flow of rivers and streams; like the sun and moon, they end but to begin anew; like the four seasons, they pass away but to return once more.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There are not more than five musical...

sections.Verse: There are not more than five musical notes, yet the combinations of these five give rise to more melodies than can ever be heard.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There are not more than five primary...

sections.Verse: There are not more than five primary colours (blue, yellow, red, white, and black), yet in combination they produce more hues than can ever be seen. 9 There are not more than five cardinal tastes (sour, acrid, salt, sweet, bitter), yet combinations of them yield more flavours than can ever be tasted.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In battle, there are not more than...

sections.Verse: In battle, there are not more than two methods of attack—the direct and the indirect; yet these two in combination give rise to an endless series of manœuvers.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The direct and the indirect lead on...

sections.Verse: The direct and the indirect lead on to each other in turn. It is like moving in a circle—you never come to an end. Who can exhaust the possibilities of their combination?

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The onset of troops is like the...

sections.Verse: The onset of troops is like the rush of a torrent which will even roll stones along in its course.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The quality of decision is like the...

sections.Verse: The quality of decision is like the well-timed swoop of a falcon which enables it to strike and destroy its victim.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore the good fighter will be terrible...

sections.Verse: Therefore the good fighter will be terrible in his onset, and prompt in his decision.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Energy may be likened to the bending...

sections.Verse: Energy may be likened to the bending of a crossbow; decision, to the releasing of the trigger.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Amid the turmoil and tumult of battle,...

sections.Verse: Amid the turmoil and tumult of battle, there may be seeming disorder and yet no real disorder at all; amid confusion and chaos, your array may be without head or tail, yet it will be proof against defeat.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Simulated disorder postulates perfect discipline; simulated fear...

sections.Verse: Simulated disorder postulates perfect discipline; simulated fear postulates courage; simulated weakness postulates strength.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hiding order beneath the cloak of disorder...

sections.Verse: Hiding order beneath the cloak of disorder is simply a question of subdivision;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus one who is skilful at keeping...

sections.Verse: Thus one who is skilful at keeping the enemy on the move maintains deceitful appearances, according to which the enemy will act.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By holding out baits, he keeps him...

sections.Verse: By holding out baits, he keeps him on the march; then with a body of picked men he lies in wait for him.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The clever combatant looks to the effect...

sections.Verse: The clever combatant looks to the effect of combined energy, and does not require too much from individuals.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When he utilises combined energy, his fighting...

sections.Verse: When he utilises combined energy, his fighting men become as it were like unto rolling logs or stones. For it is the nature of a log or stone to remain motionless on level ground, and to move when on a slope; if four-cornered, to come to a standstill, but if round-shaped, to go rolling down.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus the energy developed by good fighting...

sections.Verse: Thus the energy developed by good fighting men is as the momentum of a round stone rolled down a mountain thousands of feet in height. So much on the subject of energy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: Whoever is first in...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: Whoever is first in the field and awaits the coming of the enemy, will be fresh for the fight; whoever is second in the field and has to hasten to battle, will arrive exhausted.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore the clever combatant imposes his will...

sections.Verse: Therefore the clever combatant imposes his will on the enemy, but does not allow the enemy’s will to be imposed on him.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By holding out advantages to him, he...

sections.Verse: By holding out advantages to him, he can cause the enemy to approach of his own accord; or, by inflicting damage, he can make it impossible for the enemy to draw near.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If the enemy is taking his ease,...

sections.Verse: If the enemy is taking his ease, he can harass him;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Appear at points which the enemy must...

sections.Verse: Appear at points which the enemy must hasten to defend; march swiftly to places where you are not expected.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: An army may march great distances without...

sections.Verse: An army may march great distances without distress, if it marches through country where the enemy is not.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: You can be sure of succeeding in...

sections.Verse: You can be sure of succeeding in your attacks if you only attack places which are undefended.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence that general is skilful in attack...

sections.Verse: Hence that general is skilful in attack whose opponent does not know what to defend; and he is skilful in defence whose opponent does not know what to attack.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: O divine art of subtlety and secrecy!...

sections.Verse: O divine art of subtlety and secrecy! Through you we learn to be invisible, through you inaudible;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: You may advance and be absolutely irresistible,...

sections.Verse: You may advance and be absolutely irresistible, if you make for the enemy’s weak points; you may retire and be safe from pursuit if your movements are more rapid than those of the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If we wish to fight, the enemy...

sections.Verse: If we wish to fight, the enemy can be forced to an engagement even though he be sheltered behind a high rampart and a deep ditch. All we need do is attack some other place that he will be obliged to relieve.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If we do not wish to fight,...

sections.Verse: If we do not wish to fight, we can prevent the enemy from engaging us even though the lines of our encampment be merely traced out on the ground. All we need do is to throw something odd and unaccountable in his way.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By discovering the enemy’s dispositions and remaining...

sections.Verse: By discovering the enemy’s dispositions and remaining invisible ourselves, we can keep our forces concentrated, while the enemy’s must be divided.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: We can form a single united body,...

sections.Verse: We can form a single united body, while the enemy must split up into fractions. Hence there will be a whole pitted against separate parts of a whole, which means that we shall be many to the enemy’s few.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: And if we are able thus to...

sections.Verse: And if we are able thus to attack an inferior force with a superior one, our opponents will be in dire straits.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The spot where we intend to fight...

sections.Verse: The spot where we intend to fight must not be made known; for then the enemy will have to prepare against a possible attack at several different points;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: For should the enemy strengthen his van,...

sections.Verse: For should the enemy strengthen his van, he will weaken his rear; should he strengthen his rear, he will weaken his van; should he strengthen his left, he will weaken his right; should he strengthen his right, he will weaken his left. If he sends reinforcements everywhere, he will everywhere be weak.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Numerical weakness comes from having to prepare...

sections.Verse: Numerical weakness comes from having to prepare against possible attacks; numerical strength, from compelling our adversary to make these preparations against us.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Knowing the place and the time of...

sections.Verse: Knowing the place and the time of the coming battle, we may concentrate from the greatest distances in order to fight.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: But if neither time nor place be...

sections.Verse: But if neither time nor place be known, then the left wing will be impotent to succour the right, the right equally impotent to succour the left, the van unable to relieve the rear, or the rear to support the van. How much more so if the furthest portions of the army are anything under a hundred li apart, and even the nearest are separated by several li!

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Though according to my estimate the soldiers...

sections.Verse: Though according to my estimate the soldiers of Yüeh exceed our own in number, that shall advantage them nothing in the matter of victory. I say then that victory can be achieved.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Though the enemy be stronger in numbers,...

sections.Verse: Though the enemy be stronger in numbers, we may prevent him from fighting. Scheme so as to discover his plans and the likelihood of their success.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Rouse him, and learn the principle of...

sections.Verse: Rouse him, and learn the principle of his activity or inactivity.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Carefully compare the opposing army with your...

sections.Verse: Carefully compare the opposing army with your own, so that you may know where strength is superabundant and where it is deficient.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In making tactical dispositions, the highest pitch...

sections.Verse: In making tactical dispositions, the highest pitch you can attain is to conceal them;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: How victory may be produced for them...

sections.Verse: How victory may be produced for them out of the enemy’s own tactics—that is what the multitude cannot comprehend.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: All men can see the tactics whereby...

sections.Verse: All men can see the tactics whereby I conquer, but what none can see is the strategy out of which victory is evolved.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Do not repeat the tactics which have...

sections.Verse: Do not repeat the tactics which have gained you one victory, but let your methods be regulated by the infinite variety of circumstances.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Military tactics are like unto water; for...

sections.Verse: Military tactics are like unto water; for water in its natural course runs away from high places and hastens downwards.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: So in war, the way is to...

sections.Verse: So in war, the way is to avoid what is strong and to strike at what is weak.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Water shapes its course according to the...

sections.Verse: Water shapes its course according to the nature of the ground over which it flows; the soldier works out his victory in relation to the foe whom he is facing.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore, just as water retains no constant...

sections.Verse: Therefore, just as water retains no constant shape, so in warfare there are no constant conditions.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He who can modify his tactics in...

sections.Verse: He who can modify his tactics in relation to his opponent and thereby succeed in winning, may be called a heaven-born captain.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The five elements (water, fire, wood, metal,...

sections.Verse: The five elements (water, fire, wood, metal, earth) are not always equally predominant;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: In war, the general...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: In war, the general receives his commands from the sovereign.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Having collected an army and concentrated his...

sections.Verse: Having collected an army and concentrated his forces, he must blend and harmonise the different elements thereof before pitching his camp.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: After that, comes tactical manœuvering, than which...

sections.Verse: After that, comes tactical manœuvering, than which there is nothing more difficult.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus, to take a long and circuitous...

sections.Verse: Thus, to take a long and circuitous route, after enticing the enemy out of the way, and though starting after him, to contrive to reach the goal before him, shows knowledge of the artifice of deviation.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Manœuvering with an army is advantageous; with...

sections.Verse: Manœuvering with an army is advantageous; with an undisciplined multitude, most dangerous.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If you set a fully equipped army...

sections.Verse: If you set a fully equipped army in march in order to snatch an advantage, the chances are that you will be too late. On the other hand, to detach a flying column for the purpose involves the sacrifice of its baggage and stores.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus, if you order your men to...

sections.Verse: Thus, if you order your men to roll up their buff-coats, and make forced marches without halting day or night, covering double the usual distance at a stretch,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The stronger men will be in front,...

sections.Verse: The stronger men will be in front, the jaded ones will fall behind, and on this plan only one-tenth of your army will reach its destination.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If you march fifty li in order...

sections.Verse: If you march fifty li in order to outmanœuver the enemy, you will lose the leader of your first division, and only half your force will reach the goal.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If you march thirty li with the...

sections.Verse: If you march thirty li with the same object, two-thirds of your army will arrive.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: We may take it then that an...

sections.Verse: We may take it then that an army without its baggage-train is lost; without provisions it is lost; without bases of supply it is lost.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: We cannot enter into alliances until we...

sections.Verse: We cannot enter into alliances until we are acquainted with the designs of our neighbours.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: We are not fit to lead an...

sections.Verse: We are not fit to lead an army on the march unless we are familiar with the face of the country—its mountains and forests, its pitfalls and precipices, its marshes and swamps.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: We shall be unable to turn natural...

sections.Verse: We shall be unable to turn natural advantages to account unless we make use of local guides.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In war, practise dissimulation, and you will...

sections.Verse: In war, practise dissimulation, and you will succeed.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Whether to concentrate or to divide your...

sections.Verse: Whether to concentrate or to divide your troops, must be decided by circumstances.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Let your rapidity be that of the...

sections.Verse: Let your rapidity be that of the wind,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In raiding and plundering be like fire,

sections.Verse: In raiding and plundering be like fire,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Let your plans be dark and impenetrable...

sections.Verse: Let your plans be dark and impenetrable as night, and when you move, fall like a thunderbolt.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you plunder a countryside, let the...

sections.Verse: When you plunder a countryside, let the spoil be divided amongst your men;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ponder and deliberate before you make a...

sections.Verse: Ponder and deliberate before you make a move.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He will conquer who has learnt the...

sections.Verse: He will conquer who has learnt the artifice of deviation.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The Book of Army Management says:

sections.Verse: The Book of Army Management says:

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Gongs and drums, banners and flags, are...

sections.Verse: Gongs and drums, banners and flags, are means whereby the ears and eyes of the host may be focussed on one particular point.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The host thus forming a single united...

sections.Verse: The host thus forming a single united body, is it impossible either for the brave to advance alone, or for the cowardly to retreat alone.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In night-fighting, then, make much use of...

sections.Verse: In night-fighting, then, make much use of signal-fires and drums, and in fighting by day, of flags and banners, as a means of influencing the ears and eyes of your army.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: A whole army may be robbed of...

sections.Verse: A whole army may be robbed of its spirit;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now a soldier’s spirit is keenest in...

sections.Verse: Now a soldier’s spirit is keenest in the morning;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: A clever general, therefore, avoids an army...

sections.Verse: A clever general, therefore, avoids an army when its spirit is keen, but attacks it when it is sluggish and inclined to return. This is the art of studying moods.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Disciplined and calm, to await the appearance...

sections.Verse: Disciplined and calm, to await the appearance of disorder and hubbub amongst the enemy:—this is the art of retaining self-possession.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To be near the goal while the...

sections.Verse: To be near the goal while the enemy is still far from it, to wait at ease while the enemy is toiling and struggling, to be well-fed while the enemy is famished:—this is the art of husbanding one’s strength.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To refrain from intercepting an enemy whose...

sections.Verse: To refrain from intercepting an enemy whose banners are in perfect order, to refrain from attacking an army drawn up in calm and confident array:—this is the art of studying circumstances.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is a military axiom not to...

sections.Verse: It is a military axiom not to advance uphill against the enemy, nor to oppose him when he comes downhill.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Do not pursue an enemy who simulates...

sections.Verse: Do not pursue an enemy who simulates flight; do not attack soldiers whose temper is keen.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Do not swallow a bait offered by...

sections.Verse: Do not swallow a bait offered by the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you surround an army, leave an...

sections.Verse: When you surround an army, leave an outlet free.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Such is the art of warfare.

sections.Verse: Such is the art of warfare.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: In war, the general...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: In war, the general receives his commands from the sovereign, collects his army and concentrates his forces.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When in difficult country, do not encamp....

sections.Verse: When in difficult country, do not encamp. In country where high roads intersect, join hands with your allies. Do not linger in dangerously isolated positions.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There are roads which must not be...

sections.Verse: There are roads which must not be followed,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The general who thoroughly understands the advantages...

sections.Verse: The general who thoroughly understands the advantages that accompany variation of tactics knows how to handle his troops.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The general who does not understand these,...

sections.Verse: The general who does not understand these, may be well acquainted with the configuration of the country, yet he will not be able to turn his knowledge to practical account.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: So, the student of war who is...

sections.Verse: So, the student of war who is unversed in the art of war of varying his plans, even though he be acquainted with the Five Advantages, will fail to make the best use of his men.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence in the wise leader’s plans, considerations...

sections.Verse: Hence in the wise leader’s plans, considerations of advantage and of disadvantage will be blended together.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If our expectation of advantage be tempered...

sections.Verse: If our expectation of advantage be tempered in this way, we may succeed in accomplishing the essential part of our schemes.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If, on the other hand, in the...

sections.Verse: If, on the other hand, in the midst of difficulties we are always ready to seize an advantage, we may extricate ourselves from misfortune.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Reduce the hostile chiefs by inflicting damage...

sections.Verse: Reduce the hostile chiefs by inflicting damage on them;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The art of war teaches us to...

sections.Verse: The art of war teaches us to rely not on the likelihood of the enemy’s not coming, but on our own readiness to receive him; not on the chance of his not attacking, but rather on the fact that we have made our position unassailable.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There are five dangerous faults which may...

sections.Verse: There are five dangerous faults which may affect a general: (1) Recklessness, which leads to destruction;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These are the five besetting sins of...

sections.Verse: These are the five besetting sins of a general, ruinous to the conduct of war.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When an army is overthrown and its...

sections.Verse: When an army is overthrown and its leader slain, the cause will surely be found among these five dangerous faults. Let them be a subject of meditation.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: We come now to...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: We come now to the question of encamping the army, and observing signs of the enemy. Pass quickly over mountains, and keep in the neighbourhood of valleys.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Camp in high places,

sections.Verse: Camp in high places,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: After crossing a river, you should get...

sections.Verse: After crossing a river, you should get far away from it.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When an invading force crosses a river...

sections.Verse: When an invading force crosses a river in its onward march, do not advance to meet it in mid-stream. It will be best to let half the army get across, and then deliver your attack.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If you are anxious to fight, you...

sections.Verse: If you are anxious to fight, you should not go to meet the invader near a river which he has to cross.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Moor your craft higher up than the...

sections.Verse: Moor your craft higher up than the enemy, and facing the sun.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In crossing salt-marshes, your sole concern should...

sections.Verse: In crossing salt-marshes, your sole concern should be to get over them quickly, without any delay.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If forced to fight in a salt-marsh,...

sections.Verse: If forced to fight in a salt-marsh, you should have water and grass near you, and get your back to a clump of trees.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In dry, level country, take up an...

sections.Verse: In dry, level country, take up an easily accessible position with rising ground to your right and on your rear,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These are the four useful branches of...

sections.Verse: These are the four useful branches of military knowledge

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: All armies prefer high ground to low,

sections.Verse: All armies prefer high ground to low,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If you are careful of your men,

sections.Verse: If you are careful of your men,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you come to a hill or...

sections.Verse: When you come to a hill or a bank, occupy the sunny side, with the slope on your right rear. Thus you will at once act for the benefit of your soldiers and utilise the natural advantages of the ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When, in consequence of heavy rains up-country,...

sections.Verse: When, in consequence of heavy rains up-country, a river which you wish to ford is swollen and flecked with foam, you must wait until it subsides.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Country in which there are precipitous cliffs...

sections.Verse: Country in which there are precipitous cliffs with torrents running between, deep natural hollows, The latter defined as "places enclosed on every side by steep banks, with pools of water at the bottom." confined places,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: While we keep away from such places,...

sections.Verse: While we keep away from such places, we should get the enemy to approach them; while we face them, we should let the enemy have them on his rear.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If in the neighbourhood of your camp...

sections.Verse: If in the neighbourhood of your camp there should be any hilly country, ponds surrounded by aquatic grass, hollow basins filled with reeds, or woods with thick undergrowth, they must be carefully routed out and searched; for these are places where men in ambush or insidious spies are likely to be lurking.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the enemy is close at hand...

sections.Verse: When the enemy is close at hand and remains quiet, he is relying on the natural strength of his position.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When he keeps aloof and tries to...

sections.Verse: When he keeps aloof and tries to provoke a battle, he is anxious for the other side to advance.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If his place of encampment is easy...

sections.Verse: If his place of encampment is easy of access, he is tendering a bait.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Movement amongst the trees of a forest...

sections.Verse: Movement amongst the trees of a forest shows that the enemy is advancing.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The rising of birds in their flight...

sections.Verse: The rising of birds in their flight is the sign of an ambuscade.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When there is dust rising in a...

sections.Verse: When there is dust rising in a high column, it is the sign of chariots advancing; when the dust is low, but spread over a wide area, it betokens the approach of infantry.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Humble words and increased preparations are signs...

sections.Verse: Humble words and increased preparations are signs that the enemy is about to advance.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the light chariots come out first...

sections.Verse: When the light chariots come out first and take up a position on the wings, it is a sign that the enemy is forming for battle.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Peace proposals unaccompanied by a sworn covenant...

sections.Verse: Peace proposals unaccompanied by a sworn covenant indicate a plot.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When there is much running about

sections.Verse: When there is much running about

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When some are seen advancing and some...

sections.Verse: When some are seen advancing and some retreating, it is a lure.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the soldiers stand leaning on their...

sections.Verse: When the soldiers stand leaning on their spears, they are faint from want of food.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If those who are sent to draw...

sections.Verse: If those who are sent to draw water begin by drinking themselves, the army is suffering from thirst.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If the enemy sees an advantage to...

sections.Verse: If the enemy sees an advantage to be gained and makes no effort to secure it, the soldiers are exhausted.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If birds gather on any spot, it...

sections.Verse: If birds gather on any spot, it is unoccupied.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If there is disturbance in the camp,...

sections.Verse: If there is disturbance in the camp, the general’s authority is weak. If the banners and flags are shifted about, sedition is afoot. If the officers are angry, it means that the men are weary.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When an army feeds its horses with...

sections.Verse: When an army feeds its horses with grain and kills its cattle for food,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The sight of men whispering together in...

sections.Verse: The sight of men whispering together in small knots or speaking in subdued tones points to disaffection amongst the rank and file.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Too frequent rewards signify that the enemy...

sections.Verse: Too frequent rewards signify that the enemy is at the end of his resources;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To begin by bluster, but afterwards to...

sections.Verse: To begin by bluster, but afterwards to take fright at the enemy’s numbers, shows a supreme lack of intelligence.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When envoys are sent with compliments in...

sections.Verse: When envoys are sent with compliments in their mouths, it is a sign that the enemy wishes for a truce.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If the enemy’s troops march up angrily...

sections.Verse: If the enemy’s troops march up angrily and remain facing ours for a long time without either joining battle or taking themselves off again, the situation is one that demands great vigilance and circumspection.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If our troops are no more in...

sections.Verse: If our troops are no more in number than the enemy, that is amply sufficient; it only means that no direct attack can be made.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He who exercises no forethought but makes...

sections.Verse: He who exercises no forethought but makes light of his opponents is sure to be captured by them.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If soldiers are punished before they have...

sections.Verse: If soldiers are punished before they have grown attached to you, they will not prove submissive; and, unless submissive, then will be practically useless. If, when the soldiers have become attached to you, punishments are not enforced, they will still be useless.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore soldiers must be treated in the...

sections.Verse: Therefore soldiers must be treated in the first instance with humanity, but kept under control by means of iron discipline.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If in training soldiers commands are habitually...

sections.Verse: If in training soldiers commands are habitually enforced, the army will be well-disciplined; if not, its discipline will be bad.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If a general shows confidence in his...

sections.Verse: If a general shows confidence in his men but always insists on his orders being obeyed,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: We may distinguish six...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: We may distinguish six kinds of terrain, to wit: (1) Accessible ground;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground which can be freely traversed by...

sections.Verse: Ground which can be freely traversed by both sides is called accessible.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: With regard to ground of this nature,...

sections.Verse: With regard to ground of this nature, be before the enemy in occupying the raised and sunny spots, and carefully guard your line of supplies.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground which can be abandoned but is...

sections.Verse: Ground which can be abandoned but is hard to re-occupy is called entangling.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: From a position of this sort, if...

sections.Verse: From a position of this sort, if the enemy is unprepared, you may sally forth and defeat him. But if the enemy is prepared for your coming, and you fail to defeat him, then, return being impossible, disaster will ensue.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the position is such that neither...

sections.Verse: When the position is such that neither side will gain by making the first move, it is called temporising ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In a position of this sort, even...

sections.Verse: In a position of this sort, even though the enemy should offer us an attractive bait,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: With regard to narrow passes, if you...

sections.Verse: With regard to narrow passes, if you can occupy them first, let them be strongly garrisoned and await the advent of the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Should the enemy forestall you in occupying...

sections.Verse: Should the enemy forestall you in occupying a pass, do not go after him if the pass is fully garrisoned, but only if it is weakly garrisoned.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: With regard to precipitous heights, if you...

sections.Verse: With regard to precipitous heights, if you are beforehand with your adversary, you should occupy the raised and sunny spots, and there wait for him to come up.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If the enemy has occupied them before...

sections.Verse: If the enemy has occupied them before you, do not follow him, but retreat and try to entice him away.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If you are situated at a great...

sections.Verse: If you are situated at a great distance from the enemy, and the strength of the two armies is equal, it is not easy to provoke a battle,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These six are the principles connected with...

sections.Verse: These six are the principles connected with Earth.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now an army is exposed to six...

sections.Verse: Now an army is exposed to six several calamities, not arising from natural causes, but from faults for which the general is responsible. These are: (1) Flight; (2) insubordination; (3) collapse; (4) ruin; (5) disorganisation; (6) rout.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Other conditions being equal, if one force...

sections.Verse: Other conditions being equal, if one force is hurled against another ten times its size, the result will be the flight of the former.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the common soldiers are too strong...

sections.Verse: When the common soldiers are too strong and their officers too weak, the result is insubordination.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the higher officers are angry and...

sections.Verse: When the higher officers are angry and insubordinate, and on meeting the enemy give battle on their own account from a feeling of resentment, before the commander-in-chief can tell whether or no he is in a position to fight, the result is ruin.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the general is weak and without...

sections.Verse: When the general is weak and without authority; when his orders are not clear and distinct;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When a general, unable to estimate the...

sections.Verse: When a general, unable to estimate the enemy’s strength, allows an inferior force to engage a larger one, or hurls a weak detachment against a powerful one, and neglects to place picked soldiers in the front rank, the result must be a rout.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: These are six ways of courting defeat,...

sections.Verse: These are six ways of courting defeat, which must be carefully noted by the general who has attained a responsible post.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The natural formation of the country is...

sections.Verse: The natural formation of the country is the soldier’s best ally;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He who knows these things, and in...

sections.Verse: He who knows these things, and in fighting puts his knowledge into practice, will win his battles. He who knows them not, nor practises them, will surely be defeated.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If fighting is sure to result in...

sections.Verse: If fighting is sure to result in victory, then you must fight, even though the ruler forbid it; if fighting will not result in victory, then you must not fight even at the ruler’s bidding.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The general who advances without coveting fame...

sections.Verse: The general who advances without coveting fame and retreats without fearing disgrace,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Regard your soldiers as your children, and...

sections.Verse: Regard your soldiers as your children, and they will follow you into the deepest valleys; look on them as your own beloved sons, and they will stand by you even unto death.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If, however, you are indulgent, but unable...

sections.Verse: If, however, you are indulgent, but unable to make your authority felt; kind-hearted, but unable to enforce your commands; and incapable, moreover, of quelling disorder: then your soldiers must be likened to spoilt children; they are useless for any practical purpose.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If we know that our own men...

sections.Verse: If we know that our own men are in a condition to attack, but are unaware that the enemy is not open to attack, we have gone only halfway towards victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If we know that the enemy is...

sections.Verse: If we know that the enemy is open to attack, but are unaware that our own men are not in a condition to attack, we have gone only halfway towards victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If we know that the enemy is...

sections.Verse: If we know that the enemy is open to attack, and also know that our men are in a condition to attack, but are unaware that the nature of the ground makes fighting impracticable, we have still gone only halfway towards victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the experienced soldier, once in motion,...

sections.Verse: Hence the experienced soldier, once in motion, is never bewildered; once he has broken camp, he is never at a loss.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the saying: If you know the...

sections.Verse: Hence the saying: If you know the enemy and know yourself, your victory will not stand in doubt; if you know Heaven and know Earth, you may make your victory complete.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: The art of war...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: The art of war recognises nine varieties of ground: (1) Dispersive ground; (2) facile ground; (3) contentious ground; (4) open ground; (5) ground of intersecting highways; (6) serious ground; (7) difficult ground; (8) hemmed-in ground; (9) desperate ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When a chieftain is fighting in his...

sections.Verse: When a chieftain is fighting in his own territory, it is dispersive ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When he has penetrated into hostile territory,...

sections.Verse: When he has penetrated into hostile territory, but to no great distance, it is facile ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground the possession of which imports great...

sections.Verse: Ground the possession of which imports great advantage to either side, is contentious ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground on which each side has liberty...

sections.Verse: Ground on which each side has liberty of movement is open ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground which forms the key to three...

sections.Verse: Ground which forms the key to three contiguous states,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When an army has penetrated into the...

sections.Verse: When an army has penetrated into the heart of a hostile country, leaving a number of fortified cities in its rear, it is serious ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Mountain forests,

sections.Verse: Mountain forests,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground which is reached through narrow gorges,...

sections.Verse: Ground which is reached through narrow gorges, and from which we can only retire by tortuous paths, so that a small number of the enemy would suffice to crush a large body of our men: this is hemmed in ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Ground on which we can only be...

sections.Verse: Ground on which we can only be saved from destruction by fighting without delay, is desperate ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On dispersive ground, therefore, fight not. On...

sections.Verse: On dispersive ground, therefore, fight not. On facile ground, halt not. On contentious ground, attack not.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On open ground, do not try to...

sections.Verse: On open ground, do not try to block the enemy’s way.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On serious ground, gather in plunder.

sections.Verse: On serious ground, gather in plunder.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On hemmed-in ground, resort to stratagem.

sections.Verse: On hemmed-in ground, resort to stratagem.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Those who were called skilful leaders of...

sections.Verse: Those who were called skilful leaders of old knew how to drive a wedge between the enemy’s front and rear;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When the enemy’s men were scattered, they...

sections.Verse: When the enemy’s men were scattered, they prevented them from concentrating; even when their forces were united, they managed to keep them in disorder.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When it was to their advantage, they...

sections.Verse: When it was to their advantage, they made a forward move; when otherwise, they stopped still.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If asked how to cope with a...

sections.Verse: If asked how to cope with a great host of the enemy in orderly array and on the point of marching to the attack, I should say: "Begin by seizing something which your opponent holds dear; then he will be amenable to your will."

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Rapidity is the essence of war:

sections.Verse: Rapidity is the essence of war:

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The following are the principles to be...

sections.Verse: The following are the principles to be observed by an invading force: The further you penetrate into a country, the greater will be the solidarity of your troops, and thus the defenders will not prevail against you.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Make forays in fertile country in order...

sections.Verse: Make forays in fertile country in order to supply your army with food.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Carefully study the well-being of your men,

sections.Verse: Carefully study the well-being of your men,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Throw your soldiers into positions whence there...

sections.Verse: Throw your soldiers into positions whence there is no escape, and they will prefer death to flight. If they will face death, there is nothing they may not achieve.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Soldiers when in desperate straits lose the...

sections.Verse: Soldiers when in desperate straits lose the sense of fear. If there is no place of refuge, they will stand firm. If they are in the heart of a hostile country, they will show a stubborn front. If there is no help for it, they will fight hard.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus, without waiting to be marshalled, the...

sections.Verse: Thus, without waiting to be marshalled, the soldiers will be constantly on the qui vive; without waiting to be asked, they will do your will;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Prohibit the taking of omens, and do...

sections.Verse: Prohibit the taking of omens, and do away with superstitious doubts. Then, until death itself comes, no calamity need be feared.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If our soldiers are not overburdened with...

sections.Verse: If our soldiers are not overburdened with money, it is not because they have a distaste for riches; if their lives are not unduly long, it is not because they are disinclined to longevity.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On the day they are ordered out...

sections.Verse: On the day they are ordered out to battle, your soldiers may weep,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The skilful tactician may be likened to...

sections.Verse: The skilful tactician may be likened to the shuai-jan. Now the shuai-jan is a snake that is found in the Ch‘ang mountains.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Asked if an army can be made...

sections.Verse: Asked if an army can be made to imitate the shuai-jan,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence it is not enough to put...

sections.Verse: Hence it is not enough to put one’s trust in the tethering of horses, and the burying of chariot wheels in the ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The principle on which to manage an...

sections.Verse: The principle on which to manage an army is to set up one standard of courage which all must reach.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: How to make the best of both...

sections.Verse: How to make the best of both strong and weak—that is a question involving the proper use of ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus the skilful general conducts his army...

sections.Verse: Thus the skilful general conducts his army just as though he were leading a single man, willy-nilly, by the hand.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is the business of a general...

sections.Verse: It is the business of a general to be quiet and thus ensure secrecy; upright and just, and thus maintain order.
