metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He must be able to mystify his...

sections.Verse: He must be able to mystify his officers and men by false reports and appearances,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By altering his arrangements and changing his...

sections.Verse: By altering his arrangements and changing his plans,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: At the critical moment, the leader of...

sections.Verse: At the critical moment, the leader of an army acts like one who has climbed up a height and then kicks away the ladder behind him. He carries his men deep into hostile territory before he shows his hand.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: He burns his boats and breaks his...

sections.Verse: He burns his boats and breaks his cooking-pots; like a shepherd driving a flock of sheep, he drives his men this way and that, and none knows whither he is going.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To muster his host and bring it...

sections.Verse: To muster his host and bring it into danger:—this may be termed the business of the general.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The different measures suited to the nine...

sections.Verse: The different measures suited to the nine varieties of ground;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When invading hostile territory, the general principle...

sections.Verse: When invading hostile territory, the general principle is, that penetrating deeply brings cohesion; penetrating but a short way means dispersion.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you leave your own country behind,...

sections.Verse: When you leave your own country behind, and take your army across neighbourhood territory, you find yourself on critical ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you penetrate deeply into a country,...

sections.Verse: When you penetrate deeply into a country, it is serious ground. When you penetrate but a little way, it is facile ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When you have the enemy’s strongholds on...

sections.Verse: When you have the enemy’s strongholds on your rear, and narrow passes in front, it is hemmed-in ground. When there is no place of refuge at all, it is desperate ground.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Therefore, on dispersive ground, I would inspire...

sections.Verse: Therefore, on dispersive ground, I would inspire my men with unity of purpose.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On contentious ground, I would hurry up...

sections.Verse: On contentious ground, I would hurry up my rear.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On open ground, I would keep a...

sections.Verse: On open ground, I would keep a vigilant eye on my defences. On ground of intersecting highways, I would consolidate my alliances.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On serious ground, I would try to...

sections.Verse: On serious ground, I would try to ensure a continuous stream of supplies.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On hemmed-in ground, I would block any...

sections.Verse: On hemmed-in ground, I would block any way of retreat.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: For it is the soldier’s disposition to...

sections.Verse: For it is the soldier’s disposition to offer an obstinate resistance when surrounded, to fight hard when he cannot help himself, and to obey promptly when he has fallen into danger.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: We cannot enter into alliance with neighbouring...

sections.Verse: We cannot enter into alliance with neighbouring princes until we are acquainted with their designs. We are not fit to lead an army on the march unless we are familiar with the face of the country—its mountains and forests, its pitfalls and precipices, its marshes and swamps. We shall be unable to turn natural advantages to account unless we make use of local guides.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: To be ignorant of any one of...

sections.Verse: To be ignorant of any one of the following four or five principles does not befit a warlike prince.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When a warlike prince attacks a powerful...

sections.Verse: When a warlike prince attacks a powerful state, his generalship shows itself in preventing the concentration of the enemy’s forces. He overawes his opponents, and their allies are prevented from joining against him.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence he does not strive to ally...

sections.Verse: Hence he does not strive to ally himself with all and sundry, nor does he foster the power of other states. He carries out his own secret designs, keeping his antagonists in awe.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Bestow rewards without regard to rule,

sections.Verse: Bestow rewards without regard to rule,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Confront your soldiers with the deed itself;...

sections.Verse: Confront your soldiers with the deed itself; never let them know your design.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Place your army in deadly peril, and...

sections.Verse: Place your army in deadly peril, and it will survive; plunge it into desperate straits, and it will come off in safety.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: For it is precisely when a force...

sections.Verse: For it is precisely when a force has fallen into harm’s way that is capable of striking a blow for victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Success in warfare is gained by carefully...

sections.Verse: Success in warfare is gained by carefully accommodating ourselves to the enemy’s purpose.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By persistently hanging on the enemy’s flank,

sections.Verse: By persistently hanging on the enemy’s flank,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: This is called ability to accomplish a...

sections.Verse: This is called ability to accomplish a thing by sheer cunning.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: On the day that you take up...

sections.Verse: On the day that you take up your command, block the frontier passes, destroy the official tallies,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Be stern in the council-chamber,

sections.Verse: Be stern in the council-chamber,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If the enemy leaves a door open,...

sections.Verse: If the enemy leaves a door open, you must rush in.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Forestall your opponent by seizing what he...

sections.Verse: Forestall your opponent by seizing what he holds dear,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Walk in the path defined by rule,

sections.Verse: Walk in the path defined by rule,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: At first, then, exhibit the coyness of...

sections.Verse: At first, then, exhibit the coyness of a maiden, until the enemy gives you an opening; afterwards emulate the rapidity of a running hare, and it will be too late for the enemy to oppose you.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: There are five ways...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: There are five ways of attacking with fire. The first is to burn soldiers in their camp;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In order to carry out an attack,...

sections.Verse: In order to carry out an attack, we must have means available.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: There is a proper season for making...

sections.Verse: There is a proper season for making attacks with fire, and special days for starting a conflagration.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The proper season is when the weather...

sections.Verse: The proper season is when the weather is very dry; the special days are those when the moon is in the constellations of the Sieve, the Wall, the Wing or the Cross-bar;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In attacking with fire, one should be...

sections.Verse: In attacking with fire, one should be prepared to meet five possible developments:

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (1) When fire breaks out inside the...

sections.Verse: (1) When fire breaks out inside the enemy’s camp, respond at once with an attack from without.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (2) If there is an outbreak of...

sections.Verse: (2) If there is an outbreak of fire, but the enemy’s soldiers remain quiet, bide your time and do not attack.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (3) When the force of the flames...

sections.Verse: (3) When the force of the flames has reached its height, follow it up with an attack, if that is practicable; if not, stay where you are.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: (4) If it is possible to make...

sections.Verse: (4) If it is possible to make an assault with fire from without, do not wait for it to break out within, but deliver your attack at a favourable moment.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: A wind that rises in the daytime...

sections.Verse: A wind that rises in the daytime lasts long, but a night breeze soon falls.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: In every army, the five developments connected...

sections.Verse: In every army, the five developments connected with fire must be known, the movements of the stars calculated, and a watch kept for the proper days.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence those who use fire as an...

sections.Verse: Hence those who use fire as an aid to the attack show intelligence; those who use water as an aid to the attack gain an accession of strength.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: By means of water, an enemy may...

sections.Verse: By means of water, an enemy may be intercepted, but not robbed of all his belongings.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Unhappy is the fate of one who...

sections.Verse: Unhappy is the fate of one who tries to win his battles and succeed in his attacks without cultivating the spirit of enterprise; for the result is waste of time and general stagnation.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the saying: The enlightened ruler lays...

sections.Verse: Hence the saying: The enlightened ruler lays his plans well ahead; the good general cultivates his resources.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Move not unless you see an advantage;...

sections.Verse: Move not unless you see an advantage; use not your troops unless there is something to be gained; fight not unless the position is critical.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: No ruler should put troops into the...

sections.Verse: No ruler should put troops into the field merely to gratify his own spleen; no general should fight a battle simply out of pique.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If it is to your advantage, make...

sections.Verse: If it is to your advantage, make a forward move; if not, stay where you are.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Anger may in time change to gladness;...

sections.Verse: Anger may in time change to gladness; vexation may be succeeded by content.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: But a kingdom that has once been...

sections.Verse: But a kingdom that has once been destroyed can never come again into being;

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the enlightened ruler is heedful, and...

sections.Verse: Hence the enlightened ruler is heedful, and the good general full of caution. This is the way to keep a country at peace and an army intact.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Sun Tzŭ said: Raising a host of...

sections.Verse: Sun Tzŭ said: Raising a host of a hundred thousand men and marching them great distances entails heavy loss on the people and a drain on the resources of the State. The daily expenditure will amount to a thousand ounces of silver.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hostile armies may face each other for...

sections.Verse: Hostile armies may face each other for years, striving for the victory which is decided in a single day. This being so, to remain in ignorance of the enemy’s condition simply because one grudges the outlay of a hundred ounces of silver in honours and emoluments,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: One who acts thus is no leader...

sections.Verse: One who acts thus is no leader of men, no present help to his sovereign, no master of victory.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Thus, what enables the wise sovereign and...

sections.Verse: Thus, what enables the wise sovereign and the good general to strike and conquer, and achieve things beyond the reach of ordinary men, is foreknowledge.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Now this foreknowledge cannot be elicited from...

sections.Verse: Now this foreknowledge cannot be elicited from spirits; it cannot be obtained inductively from experience,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Knowledge of the enemy’s dispositions can only...

sections.Verse: Knowledge of the enemy’s dispositions can only be obtained from other men.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence the use of spies, of whom...

sections.Verse: Hence the use of spies, of whom there are five classes: (1) Local spies; (2) inward spies; (3) converted spies; (4) doomed spies; (5) surviving spies.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: When these five kinds of spy are...

sections.Verse: When these five kinds of spy are all at work, none can discover the secret system. This is called "divine manipulation of the threads." It is the sovereign’s most precious faculty.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Having local spies means employing the services...

sections.Verse: Having local spies means employing the services of the inhabitants of a district.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Having inward spies, making use of officials...

sections.Verse: Having inward spies, making use of officials of the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Having converted spies, getting hold of the...

sections.Verse: Having converted spies, getting hold of the enemy’s spies and using them for our own purposes.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Having doomed spies, doing certain things openly...

sections.Verse: Having doomed spies, doing certain things openly for purposes of deception, and allowing our own spies to know of them and report them to the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence it is that with none in...

sections.Verse: Hence it is that with none in the whole army are more intimate relations to be maintained than with spies.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Spies cannot be usefully employed without a...

sections.Verse: Spies cannot be usefully employed without a certain intuitive sagacity.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: They cannot be properly managed without benevolence...

sections.Verse: They cannot be properly managed without benevolence and straightforwardness.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Without subtle ingenuity of mind, one cannot...

sections.Verse: Without subtle ingenuity of mind, one cannot make certain of the truth of their reports.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Be subtle! be subtle! and use your...

sections.Verse: Be subtle! be subtle! and use your spies for every kind of business.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: If a secret piece of news is...

sections.Verse: If a secret piece of news is divulged by a spy before the time is ripe, he must be put to death together with the man to whom the secret was told.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Whether the object be to crush an...

sections.Verse: Whether the object be to crush an army, to storm a city, or to assassinate an individual, it is always necessary to begin by finding out the names of the attendants, the aides-de- camp,

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The enemy’s spies who have come to...

sections.Verse: The enemy’s spies who have come to spy on us must be sought out, tempted with bribes, led away and comfortably housed. Thus they will become converted spies and available for our service.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is through the information brought by...

sections.Verse: It is through the information brought by the converted spy that we are able to acquire and employ local and inward spies.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: It is owing to his information, again,...

sections.Verse: It is owing to his information, again, that we can cause the doomed spy to carry false tidings to the enemy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Lastly, it is by his information that...

sections.Verse: Lastly, it is by his information that the surviving spy can be used on appointed occasions.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: The end and aim of spying in...

sections.Verse: The end and aim of spying in all its five varieties is knowledge of the enemy; and this knowledge can only be derived, in the first instance, from the converted spy.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Of old, the rise of the Yin...

sections.Verse: Of old, the rise of the Yin dynasty

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Hence it is only the enlightened ruler...

sections.Verse: Hence it is only the enlightened ruler and the wise general who will use the highest intelligence of the army for purposes of spying and thereby they achieve great results.

metadata.source: The Art of War, Sun Tzu, trans. Lionel Giles, 1910

items.name: Chapter I — Laying Plans

sections.About This Chapter: The foundation of the entire work. Sun Tzu establishes that warfare is a matter of vital importance to the State — a matter of life and death. He introduces the Five Constant Factors (Moral Law, Heaven, Earth, the Commander, Method and Discipline) and the art of strategic deception: 'All warfare is based on deception.' The chapter ends with the famous assessment method: compare the five factors between yourself and your enemy before committing to war.
sections.For Readers: Contains 21 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter II — Waging War

sections.About This Chapter: The economics of warfare. Sun Tzu argues that prolonged campaigns drain the treasury, exhaust the army, and invite opportunistic attacks. 'There is no instance of a country having benefited from prolonged warfare.' Speed and efficiency are paramount. The chapter introduces the principle of using the enemy's own resources against them — foraging off conquered territory rather than relying on supply lines.
sections.For Readers: Contains 19 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter III — Attack by Stratagem

sections.About This Chapter: The highest form of generalship is to defeat the enemy without fighting. 'Supreme excellence consists in breaking the enemy's resistance without fighting.' Sun Tzu ranks military objectives: best is to attack the enemy's strategy, next their alliances, next their army, worst is to besiege walled cities. Contains the famous: 'Know the enemy and know yourself; in a hundred battles you will never be in peril.'
sections.For Readers: Contains 18 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter IV — Tactical Dispositions

sections.About This Chapter: Defense and offense as complementary arts. The skilled commander first makes himself invulnerable, then waits for the enemy to become vulnerable. 'To secure ourselves against defeat lies in our own hands, but the opportunity of defeating the enemy is provided by the enemy himself.' Victory should be so well-prepared that it seems easy — the greatest victories appear unremarkable.
sections.For Readers: Contains 20 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter V — Energy

sections.About This Chapter: The art of using momentum and timing. Sun Tzu distinguishes between direct (zheng) and indirect (qi) forces, comparing them to the five musical notes or five primary colors — few in number, but infinite in combination. 'The onset of troops is like the rush of a torrent which will even roll stones along in its course.' Energy in warfare is about creating irresistible momentum at the decisive point.
sections.For Readers: Contains 22 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter VI — Weak Points and Strong

sections.About This Chapter: The art of exploiting weakness. Arrive first at contested ground. Attack where the enemy is unprepared. 'You can be sure of succeeding in your attacks if you only attack places which are undefended.' The chapter elaborates Sun Tzu's water metaphor: as water flows downhill, so an army should flow toward weakness and away from strength. This is the longest chapter — 34 verses on adaptability and strategic targeting.
sections.For Readers: Contains 34 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter VIII — Variation of Tactics

sections.About This Chapter: Flexibility of mind. There are roads that must not be followed, armies that must not be attacked, towns that must not be besieged. The commander must understand when to apply rules and when to break them. Sun Tzu warns against the five dangerous faults in a general: recklessness, cowardice, a hasty temper, a delicacy of honor, and over-solicitude for his men.
sections.For Readers: Contains 14 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter IX — The Army on the March

sections.About This Chapter: Practical field craft — how to read terrain, interpret signs, and understand the enemy's movements. The longest chapter in the book with 45 verses covering everything from crossing rivers to reading dust clouds. 'When the enemy is close at hand and remains quiet, he is relying on the natural strength of his position.' A masterclass in observation and environmental awareness.
sections.For Readers: Contains 45 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter X — Terrain

sections.About This Chapter: Six types of terrain and how to use them: accessible, entangling, temporizing, narrow passes, precipitous heights, and positions at great distance. Also addresses six calamities that are the fault of the general rather than nature: flight, insubordination, collapse, ruin, disorganization, and rout. 'The natural formation of the country is the soldier's best ally.'
sections.For Readers: Contains 31 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter XI — The Nine Situations

sections.About This Chapter: The most complex chapter — 68 verses on the nine varieties of ground and how strategy changes with each. From dispersive ground (fighting in your own territory) to desperate ground (where survival requires fighting). Contains the famous metaphor of the shuai-jan serpent: 'Strike at its head, and you will be attacked by its tail; strike at its tail, and you will be attacked by its head; strike at its middle, and you will be attacked by head and tail both.'
sections.For Readers: Contains 68 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter XII — The Attack by Fire

sections.About This Chapter: The uses of fire in warfare — five ways to attack with fire, five kinds of fire attack, and the conditions of wind and weather that determine success. But Sun Tzu ends this chapter with a deeper point: 'No ruler should put troops into the field merely to gratify his own spleen; no general should fight a battle simply out of pique.' War is a last resort, to be guided by reason, not passion.
sections.For Readers: Contains 21 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter XIII — The Use of Spies

sections.About This Chapter: Intelligence and espionage — the final and perhaps most important chapter. Sun Tzu identifies five kinds of spies: local, inward, converted, doomed, and surviving. 'What enables the wise sovereign and the good general to strike and conquer, and achieve things beyond the reach of ordinary men, is foreknowledge.' Knowledge, not force, is the ultimate weapon.
sections.For Readers: Contains 26 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.

items.name: Chapter VII — Manoeuvering

sections.About This Chapter: Movement and its hazards. How to turn indirect routes into direct ones, how to march through difficult terrain, how to use gongs, drums, banners, and fires to coordinate movement. 'Manoeuvering with an army is advantageous; with an undisciplined multitude, most dangerous.' Contains practical advice on when to fight and when to yield, and the warning never to press a desperate foe.
sections.For Readers: Contains 37 verses. Sun Tzu's original text, with all commentary by Lionel Giles removed.
